filePath
option.value: /ProfilePage (snapshot).jsx

option.name: originalContent
updatedContent
option.value:
import { useAuth } from "../context/AuthContext"; export default function ProfilePage() { const { user } = useAuth(); return (
{user?.username?.[0]?.toUpperCase() || 'U'}

My Profile

Manage your personal information and account security.

Account ID #{user?.userId}
Username {user?.username}
Email {user?.email}
Workspace Role {user?.role}
Verification Status {user?.isVerified ? "✓ Verified" : "⚠ Pending verification"}
); }

option.value: /UsersPage (snapshot).jsx

option.name: originalContent
updatedContent
updatedContent
option.value:
--- name: feature-google-login overview: Tích hợp xác thực Google (OAuth2) cho hệ thống, cho phép đăng nhập/đăng ký bằng One-Tap/Google Button. todos: - id: backend-google-auth content: Thêm endpoint POST /api/v1/auth/google. Nhận idToken, verify qua Google API, cấp quyền (hoặc tự tạo user) và trả session JWT. status: pending - id: frontend-google-oauth content: Cài @react-oauth/google, bọc GoogleOAuthProvider ở main.jsx, cấu hình env VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID. status: pending - id: frontend-ui content: Gắn component GoogleLogin vào LoginPage và RegisterPage, gọi context login Google. status: pending - id: learning-notes content: Tạo /learning-notes/oauth2-google-login.md để lưu lại quá trình tích hợp Auth + Concurrency Bulk Import. status: pending isProject: false ---
# Feature: Google Login Integration
## 1. Backend (Auth Service) - **Endpoint:** `POST /api/v1/auth/google` - **Logic:** 1. Nhận chuỗi `id_token` từ Frontend. 2. Gửi HTTP GET tới `https://oauth2.googleapis.com/tokeninfo?id_token={token}` để verify (hoặc dùng thư viện `google.golang.org/api/idtoken`). Cách gọi HTTP thuần sẽ nhẹ hơn. 3. Kiểm tra trường `aud` có khớp với `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` hay không (bảo mật cơ bản). 4. Lấy `email` và `name`. 5. Check user theo `email` trong DB: - Tồn tại: Log in, trả về accessToken. - Chưa tồn tại: Tự động đăng ký một user với role `member`, random password, `isVerified = true`. Log in rồi trả về accessToken.
## 2. Frontend (React) - **Thư viện:** `@react-oauth/google` - **Luồng hoạt động:** - Import `GoogleOAuthProvider`. - Ở màn hình `/login`, hiển thị nút Google. - Khi user click và chọn tài khoản Google thành công -> Google trả về một chuỗi `credential` (chính là JWT ID Token). - Dispatch API gọi Backend `POST /auth/google`. - Backend trả về giống luồng `/login` bình thường -> Lưu token, update Auth Context.
## 3. Cập nhật Learning Notes - **Thư mục:** `learning-notes/` - Thêm file `.md` tài liệu hóa kiến thức: - Oauth2 OpenID Connect (Luồng Frontend cấp Token, Backend verify). - Bulk Import CSV theo mô hình Concurrency Worker Pool.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
--- name: stage2-frontend-bulk-import overview: Triển khai giao diện frontend cho chức năng Bulk Import Users (Stage 2). Thêm nút upload CSV cho Manager trong trang UsersPage, gọi API và hiển thị kết quả xử lý. todos: - id: api-integration content: Thêm `importUsers(file)` vào `src/api/userApi.js` gọi endpoint `POST /api/v1/import-users` dạng `multipart/form-data`. status: pending - id: ui-component content: Tạo component `ImportUsersModal.jsx` (hoặc section) chứa form upload `.csv`. status: pending - id: integration-users-page content: Tích hợp component vào `src/pages/UsersPage.jsx` (hiển thị nút "Import Users" riêng cho Manager). status: pending - id: result-feedback content: Viết logic xử lý Response UI (Hiển thị số Success, Failed, bảng danh sách Errors các dòng và warning khi bị `errorsTruncated`). status: pending - id: update-docs content: Cập nhật `frontend/README.md` về tính năng import CSV ở Frontend. status: pending isProject: false ---
# Kế hoạch Frontend Stage 2: Bulk Import Users
## 1. Scope Tạo giao diện (UI) cho phép user có role `manager` upload file `.csv` chứa danh sách user mới, gọi endpoint POST `/api/v1/import-users` đã hoàn thiện ở backend, đồng thời hiển thị kết quả parse chi tiết (thành công / thất bại) trên màn hình.
## 2. Các thay đổi dự kiến
### 2.1 API Service (frontend/src/api/userApi.js) Thêm hàm `importUsers(file)`: ```javascript export const importUsers = async (file) => { const formData = new FormData(); formData.append("file", file);
// Lưu ý: với fetch API dùng FormData, cần BỎ header 'Content-Type' để browser tự tính toán boundray. // getAuthHeaders() từ tokenStorage nhưng cần loại bỏ Content-Type nếu có mặc định ... } ```
### 2.2 Component & UI (frontend/src/pages/UsersPage.jsx & Component mới) - **Nơi đặt:** Trong `UsersPage.jsx` (ManagerRoute đã bảo vệ trang này). - **UI Element:** Cạnh nút Refresh/Search, thêm nút **"Bulk Import CSV"**. - **Component `ImportUsersModal`**: - `input type="file" accept=".csv"`. - Nút "Upload". Disable nút khi đang loading hoặc chưa chọn file. - Sau khi fetch xong, thay thế form upload bằng **Biểu đồ/Thống kê ngắn**: - Số lượng user thêm thành công (`success`). - Số lượng user lỗi (`failed`). - Danh sách chi tiết lỗi: hiển thị bảng / list các `errors` gồm `rowNumber`, `email`, `message`. - Warning alert nếu `errorsTruncated` = `true`.
### 2.3 UX / Error Handling - Bắt lỗi size file ở frontend (<3MB) trước khi gọi API (tối ưu). - Hiển thị Toast (với `ToastContext`) khi upload gặp sự cố server (500) hoặc network error. - Có nút Tải lại danh sách (Refresh Users List) khi đóng Modal import/nhập xong.
### 2.4 Cập nhật tài liệu - Thêm thông tin file `frontend/README.md`. Bổ sung "Bulk Import Users UI" vào mục tiến độ Stage 2.
## 3. Quá trình kiểm thử (Verification) 1. Thử login vào account Member -> Chặn, không cho thấy trang Users / Nút Import. 2. Login Manager -> thấy nút Import. 3. Upload 1 file CSV trống, kiểm tra lỗi UI. 4. Upload file file ảnh / size lớn -> Báo lỗi. 5. Upload CSV chuẩn 10 dòng (5 đúng, 5 sai logic/trùng email) -> Xác minh UI hiện đủ số success/fail và các dòng bị reject.

option.name: originalContent
option.value:
# If you prefer the allow list template instead of the deny list, see community template: # https://github.com/github/gitignore/blob/main/community/Golang/Go.AllowList.gitignore # # Binaries for programs and plugins *.exe *.exe~ *.dll *.so *.dylib
# Test binary, built with `go test -c` *.test
# Code coverage profiles and other test artifacts *.out coverage.* *.coverprofile profile.cov
# Dependency directories (remove the comment below to include it) # vendor/
# Go workspace file go.work go.work.sum
# env file .env .env.*
# Environment files **/.env **/.env.*
# Editor/IDE # .idea/ # .vscode/
*.exe .env
# Root tool dependencies /node_modules/
# Local learning notes (do not commit) /learning-notes/

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# If you prefer the allow list template instead of the deny list, see community template: # https://github.com/github/gitignore/blob/main/community/Golang/Go.AllowList.gitignore # # Binaries for programs and plugins *.exe *.exe~ *.dll *.so *.dylib
# Test binary, built with `go test -c` *.test
# Code coverage profiles and other test artifacts *.out coverage.* *.coverprofile profile.cov
# Dependency directories (remove the comment below to include it) # vendor/
# Go workspace file go.work go.work.sum
# env file .env .env.*
# Environment files **/.env **/.env.*
# Editor/IDE # .idea/ # .vscode/
*.exe .env
# Root tool dependencies /node_modules/
# Local learning notes (do not commit) /learning-notes/
# Cursor workspace data /.cursor/

option.name: originalContent
option.value:
# Seta Golang Intern Mini Project
## Introduction
This repository contains a multi-service system for the 2026 Golang Internship Capstone. The product domain is **User, Team, and Asset Management**:
- Users can register/login and are assigned roles (`manager` or `member`) - Managers can create teams and manage memberships - Users can manage folders/notes and share assets with read/write permissions
The project is intentionally developed in stages. The codebase currently includes:
- Stage 1 foundation (Auth/User + Team services) - Stage 2 Service 1 (Asset Management & Sharing) - Stage 2 Service 2 (`POST /import-users` bulk CSV import with worker pool) is **implemented** in the Auth service - Stage 3 is **not implemented yet**
## Tech Stack
This project uses a practical stack centered on **Go + Gin + GORM + PostgreSQL + React + Vite + Docker Compose**. The core reason is not trend alignment; it is execution speed with clean boundaries for an internship capstone that must show architecture thinking, API discipline, and incremental delivery.
On the backend, Go is a strong fit because each service is network-heavy and mostly I/O-bound rather than CPU-bound. Go gives lightweight concurrency primitives, good performance, and a simple deployment model. Compared with Node.js, Go avoids runtime-level event loop bottlenecks for some workloads and provides stronger compile-time guarantees. Compared with Java/Spring, Go reduces framework complexity and startup overhead, which helps when you run multiple services locally during development and demos.
For HTTP, this project uses Gin. The choice favors predictable route/middleware handling and quick bootstrap for service-oriented REST APIs. A raw `net/http` setup would be lighter, but would require more repetitive plumbing for binding, validation, and structured handlers. Heavier frameworks could offer more batteries included, but would slow iteration and add cognitive load for an educational project.
Database access uses GORM with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL is selected because the domain needs transactional consistency (sessions, memberships, ownership, shares, revoke flows), rich indexing options, and mature tooling. A NoSQL store could simplify some document-like parts, but would complicate relational checks such as ownership + sharing + team oversight in one permission flow. GORM is a trade-off: it speeds up CRUD and migration work through model-driven code, but can hide SQL complexity. For this stage, faster iteration is more valuable than hand-optimized SQL everywhere. Where needed, repository boundaries keep room for future query tuning.
The API style is REST (JSON over HTTP). REST was chosen over GraphQL and gRPC for stage pragmatism. GraphQL is powerful for client-driven querying, but introduces schema tooling and resolver complexity that does not materially improve this project’s early phases. gRPC gives excellent typed contracts and performance, but adds additional proto/tooling overhead and less browser-native ergonomics. REST keeps onboarding and debugging straightforward with curl/Postman while still allowing clear service contracts.
Frontend uses React + React Router + Vite. React is already a standard choice for role-based UI flows and stateful forms. Vite keeps startup fast and configuration small, which matters when frontend is only one part of a larger multi-service workflow. A full framework (for example Next.js) would provide extra features (SSR, file-system routing), but those are not core requirements for this backend-focused capstone.
For environment orchestration, Docker Compose is used alongside local process scripts. Compose gives consistent multi-service startup (`db`, `auth`, `team`, `asset`, `frontend`) and lower setup friction across machines. The trade-off is additional Dockerfile and hot-reload configuration overhead. To balance this, the project supports two paths: one-command local process startup (`npm run dev:all`) and one-command Compose startup (`npm run docker:up`).
A notable architecture decision is keeping service boundaries while still using one PostgreSQL instance in current stages. This is a pragmatic compromise: it keeps deployment simple and accelerates feature delivery. The downside is tighter coupling than an event-driven or fully isolated data-plane architecture. The code addresses this by preferring service-to-service calls for authorization context (for example token validation and relationship checks) instead of direct cross-service table ownership. This creates a cleaner migration path to stricter service isolation later.
In summary, the stack prioritizes **clarity, maintainability, and staged delivery** over maximal scalability patterns. It intentionally postpones heavier choices (message queue, event sourcing, service mesh, polyglot persistence) until they are justified by real bottlenecks, which is appropriate for the current milestone.
## Requirements
- Go `1.24+` (project modules currently target Go `1.26.2`) - Node.js `18+` - npm `9+` - Docker Desktop (optional, for containerized startup) - Root environment files: - `.env.backend` - `.env.frontend`
## Project Structure
```text . ├─ frontend/ │ ├─ src/ │ │ ├─ api/ │ │ ├─ pages/ │ │ ├─ routes/ │ │ └─ context/ │ └─ README.md ├─ services/ │ ├─ auth-user-management-service/ │ ├─ team-management-service/ │ └─ asset-management-service/ ├─ project requirement & instruction/ ├─ docker-compose.yml ├─ package.json └─ README.md ```
## Dependencies
Key workspace dependencies:
- Root tooling: `concurrently` - Frontend: `react`, `react-dom`, `react-router-dom`, `vite`, `@vitejs/plugin-react` - Backend services: - HTTP: `gin` - ORM/DB: `gorm`, `gorm postgres driver` - Config: `godotenv` - Auth/session helpers: `golang-jwt/jwt` (where applicable) - API docs: `swag`, `gin-swagger`, `swaggo/files` (Swagger UI per service)
## API Documentation
Each Go service ships **Swagger 2.0** specs and **Swagger UI** (via [swaggo/swag](https://github.com/swaggo/swag) and [gin-swagger](https://github.com/swaggo/gin-swagger)):
| Service | Swagger UI (default local) | |--------|----------------------------| | Auth | `http://localhost:8080/swagger/index.html` | | Team | `http://localhost:8081/swagger/index.html` | | Asset | `http://localhost:8082/swagger/index.html` |
Use **Authorize** in Swagger UI with a value like `Bearer <your_jwt>` (the APIs expect a `Bearer` prefix in the `Authorization` header).
Regenerate specs after changing handler comments (run from each service directory):
```powershell go install github.com/swaggo/swag/cmd/swag@v1.16.4 cd services/auth-user-management-service swag init -g main.go -o docs --parseInternal -d ./cmd,./internal/handler,./internal/usecase ```
Use the same `swag init` line in `team-management-service` and `asset-management-service` (module paths differ only by `cd`).
Service-level narrative API docs are also in each service README:
- [Auth User Management README](services/auth-user-management-service/README.md) - [Team Management README](services/team-management-service/README.md) - [Asset Management README](services/asset-management-service/README.md)
## Architecture Overview
```mermaid flowchart LR Frontend["Frontend (React + Vite)"] --> AuthService["Auth Service :8080"] Frontend --> TeamService["Team Service :8081"] Frontend --> AssetService["Asset Service :8082"] TeamService --> AuthService AssetService --> AuthService AssetService --> TeamService AuthService --> Postgres["PostgreSQL :5432"] TeamService --> Postgres AssetService --> Postgres ```
## Service Catalog
- `auth-user-management-service` (`:8080`) Registration, email verification, login/logout, user listing, session validation. - `team-management-service` (`:8081`) Team creation and member/manager management with role-based restrictions. - `asset-management-service` (`:8082`) Folder/note CRUD, sharing (read/write), folder inheritance, manager read-only oversight. - `frontend` (`:5173`) UI for auth, teams, and assets, using Vite proxy to backend APIs.
## Run and Development Guide
### Option A: Local One-Command Startup
```powershell npm install npm run dev:all ```
### Option B: Docker One-Command Startup (Dev Hot Reload)
```powershell npm run docker:up ```
Useful Docker commands:
```powershell npm run docker:logs npm run docker:down ```
## Environment
### Backend (`.env.backend`)
Common keys used across services include:
- `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_USER`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `DB_NAME` - `PORT` (auth), `TEAM_SERVICE_PORT`, `ASSET_SERVICE_PORT` - `JWT_SECRET`, `JWT_EXPIRES_HOURS` - `APP_BASE_URL` - `AUTH_SERVICE_URL`, `TEAM_SERVICE_URL` - SMTP variables for email verification
### Frontend (`.env.frontend`)
- `VITE_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_TEAM_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_ASSET_API_BASE_URL`
## Current Status
- Stage 1 core foundation: implemented - Stage 2 Service 1 (assets and sharing): implemented - Stage 2 Service 2 (bulk CSV import with worker pool): implemented - Stage 3 scale/events scope: pending
This documentation reflects the current in-progress state of the internship project rather than a final production release.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Seta Golang Intern Mini Project
## Introduction
This repository contains a multi-service system for the 2026 Golang Internship Capstone. The product domain is **User, Team, and Asset Management**:
- Users can register/login and are assigned roles (`manager` or `member`) - Managers can create teams and manage memberships - Users can manage folders/notes and share assets with read/write permissions
The project is intentionally developed in stages. The codebase currently includes:
- Stage 1 foundation (Auth/User + Team services) - Stage 2 Service 1 (Asset Management & Sharing) - Stage 2 Service 2 (`POST /import-users` bulk CSV import with worker pool) is **implemented** in the Auth service - Stage 3 is **not implemented yet**
## Tech Stack
This project uses a practical stack centered on **Go + Gin + GORM + PostgreSQL + React + Vite + Docker Compose**. The core reason is not trend alignment; it is execution speed with clean boundaries for an internship capstone that must show architecture thinking, API discipline, and incremental delivery.
On the backend, Go is a strong fit because each service is network-heavy and mostly I/O-bound rather than CPU-bound. Go gives lightweight concurrency primitives, good performance, and a simple deployment model. Compared with Node.js, Go avoids runtime-level event loop bottlenecks for some workloads and provides stronger compile-time guarantees. Compared with Java/Spring, Go reduces framework complexity and startup overhead, which helps when you run multiple services locally during development and demos.
For HTTP, this project uses Gin. The choice favors predictable route/middleware handling and quick bootstrap for service-oriented REST APIs. A raw `net/http` setup would be lighter, but would require more repetitive plumbing for binding, validation, and structured handlers. Heavier frameworks could offer more batteries included, but would slow iteration and add cognitive load for an educational project.
Database access uses GORM with PostgreSQL. PostgreSQL is selected because the domain needs transactional consistency (sessions, memberships, ownership, shares, revoke flows), rich indexing options, and mature tooling. A NoSQL store could simplify some document-like parts, but would complicate relational checks such as ownership + sharing + team oversight in one permission flow. GORM is a trade-off: it speeds up CRUD and migration work through model-driven code, but can hide SQL complexity. For this stage, faster iteration is more valuable than hand-optimized SQL everywhere. Where needed, repository boundaries keep room for future query tuning.
The API style is REST (JSON over HTTP). REST was chosen over GraphQL and gRPC for stage pragmatism. GraphQL is powerful for client-driven querying, but introduces schema tooling and resolver complexity that does not materially improve this project’s early phases. gRPC gives excellent typed contracts and performance, but adds additional proto/tooling overhead and less browser-native ergonomics. REST keeps onboarding and debugging straightforward with curl/Postman while still allowing clear service contracts.
Frontend uses React + React Router + Vite. React is already a standard choice for role-based UI flows and stateful forms. Vite keeps startup fast and configuration small, which matters when frontend is only one part of a larger multi-service workflow. A full framework (for example Next.js) would provide extra features (SSR, file-system routing), but those are not core requirements for this backend-focused capstone.
For environment orchestration, Docker Compose is used alongside local process scripts. Compose gives consistent multi-service startup (`db`, `auth`, `team`, `asset`, `frontend`) and lower setup friction across machines. The trade-off is additional Dockerfile and hot-reload configuration overhead. To balance this, the project supports two paths: one-command local process startup (`npm run dev:all`) and one-command Compose startup (`npm run docker:up`).
A notable architecture decision is keeping service boundaries while still using one PostgreSQL instance in current stages. This is a pragmatic compromise: it keeps deployment simple and accelerates feature delivery. The downside is tighter coupling than an event-driven or fully isolated data-plane architecture. The code addresses this by preferring service-to-service calls for authorization context (for example token validation and relationship checks) instead of direct cross-service table ownership. This creates a cleaner migration path to stricter service isolation later.
In summary, the stack prioritizes **clarity, maintainability, and staged delivery** over maximal scalability patterns. It intentionally postpones heavier choices (message queue, event sourcing, service mesh, polyglot persistence) until they are justified by real bottlenecks, which is appropriate for the current milestone.
## Requirements
- Go `1.24+` (project modules currently target Go `1.26.2`) - Node.js `18+` - npm `9+` - Docker Desktop (optional, for containerized startup) - Root environment files: - `.env.backend` - `.env.frontend`
## Project Structure
```text . ├─ frontend/ │ ├─ src/ │ │ ├─ api/ │ │ ├─ pages/ │ │ ├─ routes/ │ │ └─ context/ │ └─ README.md ├─ services/ │ ├─ auth-user-management-service/ │ ├─ team-management-service/ │ └─ asset-management-service/ ├─ project requirement & instruction/ ├─ docker-compose.yml ├─ package.json └─ README.md ```
## Dependencies
Key workspace dependencies:
- Root tooling: `concurrently` - Frontend: `react`, `react-dom`, `react-router-dom`, `vite`, `@vitejs/plugin-react` - Backend services: - HTTP: `gin` - ORM/DB: `gorm`, `gorm postgres driver` - Config: `godotenv` - Auth/session helpers: `golang-jwt/jwt` (where applicable) - API docs: `swag`, `gin-swagger`, `swaggo/files` (Swagger UI per service)
## API Documentation
Each Go service ships **Swagger 2.0** specs and **Swagger UI** (via [swaggo/swag](https://github.com/swaggo/swag) and [gin-swagger](https://github.com/swaggo/gin-swagger)):
| Service | Swagger UI (default local) | |--------|----------------------------| | Auth | `http://localhost:8080/swagger/index.html` | | Team | `http://localhost:8081/swagger/index.html` | | Asset | `http://localhost:8082/swagger/index.html` |
Use **Authorize** in Swagger UI with a value like `Bearer <your_jwt>` (the APIs expect a `Bearer` prefix in the `Authorization` header).
Regenerate specs after changing handler comments (run from each service directory):
```powershell go install github.com/swaggo/swag/cmd/swag@v1.16.4 cd services/auth-user-management-service swag init -g main.go -o docs --parseInternal -d ./cmd,./internal/handler,./internal/usecase ```
Use the same `swag init` line in `team-management-service` and `asset-management-service` (module paths differ only by `cd`).
Service-level narrative API docs are also in each service README:
- [Auth User Management README](services/auth-user-management-service/README.md) - [Team Management README](services/team-management-service/README.md) - [Asset Management README](services/asset-management-service/README.md)
## Architecture Overview
```mermaid flowchart LR Frontend["Frontend (React + Vite)"] --> AuthService["Auth Service :8080"] Frontend --> TeamService["Team Service :8081"] Frontend --> AssetService["Asset Service :8082"] TeamService --> AuthService AssetService --> AuthService AssetService --> TeamService AuthService --> Postgres["PostgreSQL :5432"] TeamService --> Postgres AssetService --> Postgres ```
## Service Catalog
- `auth-user-management-service` (`:8080`) Registration, email verification, login/logout, user listing, session validation. - `team-management-service` (`:8081`) Team creation and member/manager management with role-based restrictions. - `asset-management-service` (`:8082`) Folder/note CRUD, sharing (read/write), folder inheritance, manager read-only oversight. - `frontend` (`:5173`) UI for auth, teams, and assets, using Vite proxy to backend APIs.
## Run and Development Guide
### Option A: Local One-Command Startup
This method starts the PostgreSQL database via Docker in the background (`db` service), and then runs the Go backend processes and Vite frontend natively on your host machine.
```powershell npm install npm run dev:all ```
*Note: If the Go services crash immediately because the database isn't fully ready yet, just terminate the terminal (Ctrl+C) and run `npm run dev:all` again. The DB container will already be up.*
### Option B: Docker One-Command Startup (Dev Hot Reload)
```powershell npm run docker:up ```
Useful Docker commands:
```powershell npm run docker:logs npm run docker:down ```
## Environment
### Backend (`.env.backend`)
Common keys used across services include:
- `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_USER`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `DB_NAME` - `PORT` (auth), `TEAM_SERVICE_PORT`, `ASSET_SERVICE_PORT` - `JWT_SECRET`, `JWT_EXPIRES_HOURS` - `APP_BASE_URL` - `AUTH_SERVICE_URL`, `TEAM_SERVICE_URL` - SMTP variables for email verification
### Frontend (`.env.frontend`)
- `VITE_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_TEAM_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_ASSET_API_BASE_URL`
## Current Status
- Stage 1 core foundation: implemented - Stage 2 Service 1 (assets and sharing): implemented - Stage 2 Service 2 (bulk CSV import with worker pool): implemented - Stage 3 scale/events scope: pending
This documentation reflects the current in-progress state of the internship project rather than a final production release.

option.name: originalContent
option.value:
# Frontend (React)
## Introduction
This frontend is a React single-page application for the internship mini-project. It provides UI flows for:
- Authentication and email verification - Team management - Asset management and sharing
The app communicates with backend services through the Vite dev proxy under `/api/v1/*`.
## Tech Stack
- React 18 - React Router v6 - Vite 5 - JavaScript (ES modules) - Fetch API with a shared request wrapper
## Requirements
- Node.js `18+` - npm `9+` - Root `.env.frontend` file - Running backend services (`auth`, `team`, `asset`) for full feature coverage
## Project Structure
```text frontend/ ├─ src/ │ ├─ api/ # API clients per domain (auth, user, team, asset) │ ├─ auth/ # token storage helpers │ ├─ components/ # reusable UI components (navbar, toasts) │ ├─ context/ # auth and toast providers │ ├─ pages/ # route-level pages │ ├─ routes/ # route guards (protected/manager) │ ├─ utils/ # validators and helpers │ ├─ App.jsx │ └─ main.jsx ├─ index.html ├─ vite.config.js └─ package.json ```
## Dependencies
Main runtime dependencies from [package.json](package.json):
- `react` - `react-dom` - `react-router-dom`
Main development dependencies:
- `vite` - `@vitejs/plugin-react`
## API Documentation
The frontend calls these backend endpoint groups via proxy:
- Auth/User service: - `/api/v1/auth/*` - `/api/v1/users/*` - Team service: - `/api/v1/teams/*` - Asset service: - `/api/v1/folders/*` - `/api/v1/notes/*` - `/api/v1/shares/*`
Frontend domain API modules:
- `src/api/authApi.js` - `src/api/userApi.js` - `src/api/teamApi.js` - `src/api/assetApi.js`
## Architecture Overview
```mermaid flowchart LR Browser["Browser"] --> Router["React Router"] Router --> Pages["Page Components"] Pages --> Context["Auth/Toast Context"] Pages --> ApiLayer["API Layer"] ApiLayer --> ViteProxy["Vite Proxy /api/v1"] ViteProxy --> AuthService["Auth Service :8080"] ViteProxy --> TeamService["Team Service :8081"] ViteProxy --> AssetService["Asset Service :8082"] ```
## Run and Development Guide
From `frontend/`:
```powershell npm install npm run dev ```
Build production bundle:
```powershell npm run build ```
Preview production bundle:
```powershell npm run preview ```
You can also run everything from root with:
```powershell npm run dev:all ```
## Environment
Expected keys in root `.env.frontend`:
- `VITE_API_BASE_URL` (default auth/user target) - `VITE_TEAM_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_ASSET_API_BASE_URL`
The values are used by [vite.config.js](vite.config.js) proxy rules.
## Current Status
- Authentication pages are available (`/login`, `/register`, `/verify-email`). - Stage 1 pages are available (`/profile`, `/users`, `/teams`). - Stage 2 Service 1 page is available (`/assets`). - UX and validation are still evolving; this is not a final production UI.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Frontend (React)
## Introduction
This frontend is a React single-page application for the internship mini-project. It provides UI flows for:
- Authentication and email verification - Team management - Asset management and sharing
The app communicates with backend services through the Vite dev proxy under `/api/v1/*`.
## Tech Stack
- React 18 - React Router v6 - Vite 5 - JavaScript (ES modules) - Fetch API with a shared request wrapper
## Requirements
- Node.js `18+` - npm `9+` - Root `.env.frontend` file - Running backend services (`auth`, `team`, `asset`) for full feature coverage
## Project Structure
```text frontend/ ├─ src/ │ ├─ api/ # API clients per domain (auth, user, team, asset) │ ├─ auth/ # token storage helpers │ ├─ components/ # reusable UI components (navbar, toasts) │ ├─ context/ # auth and toast providers │ ├─ pages/ # route-level pages │ ├─ routes/ # route guards (protected/manager) │ ├─ utils/ # validators and helpers │ ├─ App.jsx │ └─ main.jsx ├─ index.html ├─ vite.config.js └─ package.json ```
## Dependencies
Main runtime dependencies from [package.json](package.json):
- `react` - `react-dom` - `react-router-dom`
Main development dependencies:
- `vite` - `@vitejs/plugin-react`
## API Documentation
The frontend calls these backend endpoint groups via proxy:
- Auth/User service: - `/api/v1/auth/*` - `/api/v1/users/*` - Team service: - `/api/v1/teams/*` - Asset service: - `/api/v1/folders/*` - `/api/v1/notes/*` - `/api/v1/shares/*`
Frontend domain API modules:
- `src/api/authApi.js` - `src/api/userApi.js` - `src/api/teamApi.js` - `src/api/assetApi.js`
## Architecture Overview
```mermaid flowchart LR Browser["Browser"] --> Router["React Router"] Router --> Pages["Page Components"] Pages --> Context["Auth/Toast Context"] Pages --> ApiLayer["API Layer"] ApiLayer --> ViteProxy["Vite Proxy /api/v1"] ViteProxy --> AuthService["Auth Service :8080"] ViteProxy --> TeamService["Team Service :8081"] ViteProxy --> AssetService["Asset Service :8082"] ```
## Run and Development Guide
From `frontend/`:
```powershell npm install npm run dev ```
Build production bundle:
```powershell npm run build ```
Preview production bundle:
```powershell npm run preview ```
You can also run everything from root with:
```powershell npm run dev:all ```
## Environment
Expected keys in root `.env.frontend`:
- `VITE_API_BASE_URL` (default auth/user target) - `VITE_TEAM_API_BASE_URL` - `VITE_ASSET_API_BASE_URL`
The values are used by [vite.config.js](vite.config.js) proxy rules.
## Current Status
- Authentication pages are available (`/login`, `/register`, `/verify-email`). - Stage 1 pages are available (`/profile`, `/users`, `/teams`). - Stage 2 Service 1 page is available (`/assets`). - Stage 2 Bulk Import feature UI is implemented (CSV upload modal in the Users Manager view). - UX and validation are still evolving; this is not a final production UI.

updatedContent

originalContent
updatedContent
originalContent
option.value:
# Learning Notes (Local Only)
This folder is intentionally git-ignored. It is your private, detailed study guide for the codebase.
## How to Use
- Read the flow section first to understand the path of each request. - Then read the line-by-line sections for each file. - If anything is unclear, add a question or note at the end of a section and we will refine it.
## File Map
- `auth-user-management-service.md` - `team-management-service.md` - `asset-management-service.md`
## Detail Level Policy
- Each service doc includes: - End-to-end flow (HTTP -> handler -> usecase -> repository -> DB). - Line-by-line explanations for core files. - Short explanations of Go concepts used in that file (errors, loops, interfaces, goroutines if any).
## Expansion Plan
- Add more files gradually (handlers, repositories, utilities) until every function and line has a note. - Add visual flow diagrams (Mermaid) if you want.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Internship Learning Notes
This root directory contains my personal research notes and architecture trade-offs for the mini-project.
## Documentation * [Stage 1: Auth & User Management](auth-user-management-service.md) - Understanding Session storage, custom JWT generation, global role RBAC. * [Stage 1: Team Management](team-management-service.md) - Microservices architecture, calling endpoints directly from service context, membership authorization rules. * [Stage 2: Asset Management Validation](asset-management-service.md) - Nested relationship ownership verification. Concurrency mapping over items. * [Stage 2: OAuth2 Google Login Integration](oauth2-google-login.md) - How to leverage external SSO IdP natively, zero-dependency backend tokeninfo validation, auto-register flows, and React client bindings.
## How to Use
- Read the flow section first to understand the path of each request. - Then read the line-by-line sections for each file. - If anything is unclear, add a question or note at the end of a section and we will refine it.
## File Map
- `auth-user-management-service.md` - `team-management-service.md` - `asset-management-service.md` - `oauth2-google-login.md`
## Detail Level Policy
- Each service doc includes: - End-to-end flow (HTTP -> handler -> usecase -> repository -> DB). - Line-by-line explanations for core files. - Short explanations of Go concepts used in that file (errors, loops, interfaces, goroutines if any).
## Expansion Plan
- Add more files gradually (handlers, repositories, utilities) until every function and line has a note. - Add visual flow diagrams (Mermaid) if you want.

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# OAuth2 Google Login Integration
## Giới thiệu Tài liệu này ghi chú lại quá trình tích hợp Google Login (OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect) vào hệ thống dựa trên yêu cầu từ Stage 2 mở rộng.
Hệ thống cho phép đăng nhập và đăng ký qua tài khoản Google một cách tự động (One-Tap / Google Button), sử dụng cơ chế xác thực JWT chuẩn xác.
## Luồng hoạt động (Data Flow)
```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant Frontend (React) participant Google OAuth participant Backend (Go) participant Database
User->>Frontend (React): Click "Sign in with Google" Frontend (React)->>Google OAuth: Yêu cầu xác thực tài khoản Google OAuth-->>User: Hiển thị Popup xác nhận User->>Google OAuth: Chọn tài khoản Google OAuth-->>Frontend (React): Trả về `credential` (JWT ID Token) Frontend (React)->>Backend (Go): POST `/api/v1/auth/google` (Gửi ID Token) Backend (Go)->>Google OAuth: (Verify) Gọi `tokeninfo` để kiểm tra Token hợp lệ Google OAuth-->>Backend (Go): Trả về chi tiết (Email, Name, ClientID) Backend (Go)->>Backend (Go): Xác thực `aud` khớp Google Client ID của hệ thống Backend (Go)->>Database: Check theo `email` alt Đã tồn tại User Database-->>Backend (Go): Trả về User hiện tại Backend (Go)->>Database: (Update) Đảm bảo `is_verified = true` else Chưa tồn tại User Backend (Go)->>Database: Sinh User mới với mật khẩu ngẫu nhiên & log vào Role mặc định end Backend (Go)->>Database: Tạo/Lưu phiên (Session) & Sinh JWT nội bộ Backend (Go)-->>Frontend (React): Trả về LoginResponse (Cấp quyền Access Token nội bộ) Frontend (React)-->>User: Đăng nhập thành công, chuyển hướng vào `/profile` ```
## Giải pháp Backend (Zero Dependency Setup) Để giữ system nhẹ nhàng và tránh kéo theo root pkg `google.golang.org/api` khổng lồ chỉ để verify ID token, chúng ta sử dụng một helper gọn nhẹ.
Backend sẽ gửi một HTTP GET Request tới Google: `https://oauth2.googleapis.com/tokeninfo?id_token={TOKEN}`
Response trả về sẽ cung cấp `email` và `aud`. - `aud`: Cần match với biến môi trường `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`. - `email`: Validate ID và Map trực tiếp vào User database.
Nếu User log in lần đầu qua google, hệ thống sinh một tài khoản mới tự động, set thuộc tính `isVerified` thành `true` để không bị kẹt ở luồng check email truyền thống.
## Giải pháp Frontend Tích hợp `@react-oauth/google`. - Khởi tạo `<GoogleOAuthProvider clientId="...">` chèn bên ngoài <App />. - Import thẻ `<GoogleLogin />` vào các trang `LoginPage` và `RegisterPage`. - Khi thẻ trigger `onSuccess`, nhận lấy `credentialResponse.credential` và gọi API của Server chúng ta: `googleLoginApi(idToken)`.
## Các biến môi trường cần thiết cấu hình
Root `.env.backend`: - `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`: Đều phải điền giá trị được Google Cloud Console cấp quyền.
Root `.env.frontend`: - `VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`: Id Google public.
*Lưu ý: Luôn add các domain frontend (e.g., http://localhost:5173 và http://localhost:3000) vào danh sách Authorized JavaScript origins trong thẻ Credentials thuộc thư viện GCP.*

option.name: originalContent
updatedContent
originalContent

originalContent
updatedContent
originalContent

updatedContent
updatedContent
updatedContent
